title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a scientific diving project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Depths of Discovery: The Scientific Divers

texts.D:
### Sam, The Coral Reef Census
'I spent my days counting polyps and documenting bleaching,' Sam says. [51]The hardest part was the physical toll of staying neutrally buoyant in strong currents while trying to take steady photographs.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my general stress levels plummeted; the weightlessness of the water was like a reset button for my mind.[/48] [53]I learned that the ocean is a complex ledger where every change in temperature is recorded in the life of the reef.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in financial auditing helped me stay disciplined during the repetitive data collection tasks.[/50] [47]Initially, the pressure on my ears and the claustrophobic feeling of the mask were quite a shock.[/47] It was a humbling underwater mission.
texts.B:
### Elena, The Seagrass Restoration
Elena helped plant seagrass to combat carbon emissions. 'It’s like gardening in slow motion,' she recalls. [47]I hadn't expected the psychological toll of seeing the vast 'underwater deserts' where life once thrived to affect me so much initially.[/47] [54]What kept me going was the expertise of the marine biologists, who could explain the entire ecosystem from a single blade of grass.[/54] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep sense of achievement I’d feel when I saw the first small fish returning to our new meadow.[/52] [49]The experience taught me that the ocean’s health is the most accurate barometer for the future of our planet.[/49] [56]If I hadn't seen a social media post from a conservation charity, I’d never have known this opportunity existed.[/56] It was a transformative mission.
texts.C:
### Marcus, The Ghost Net Recovery
Marcus dove to remove discarded fishing nets. 'It’s dangerous but vital work,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the lack of visibility; working in murky water with sharp tools required a level of focus that was mentally exhausting.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general physical stamina and lung efficiency reached an all-time high, which was a great side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that the ocean is incredibly forgiving if we stop the active destruction we are causing.[/53] [50]I found that the crisis management skills from my career as an emergency dispatcher were perfect for staying calm when things went wrong underwater.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the smell and the weight of the rotting nets on the boat deck, which I found quite repulsive.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried I wouldn't be brave enough.
texts.A:
### Sophie, The Underwater Archeology Survey
'I helped map a shipwreck from the 18th century,' Sophie says. [49]The project made me realize that our history is being slowly reclaimed by the sea and needs urgent protection.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the project if my university hadn't offered a summer scholarship for students from non-scientific backgrounds.[/56] [54]I was moved by the camaraderie of the international team, who communicated through complex hand signals with perfect ease.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical exhaustion of the long decompression stops to be so draining initially.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of learning the precise measurement techniques underwater incredibly frustrating and difficult.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I wouldn't be 'technical' enough, but the data recording became second nature.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or psychological impact of the underwater environment?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) mentions the pressure/claustrophobia, Elena (B) the toll of seeing 'underwater deserts', and Sophie (D) the exhaustion of decompression stops.

questions.question: notes a positive mental or physical health change that resulted from the diving?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) saw his stress levels plummet, and Marcus (C) reached an all-all time high in stamina/lung efficiency.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perspective on the ocean's role as a barometer or the reclamation of history?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) reflects on the ocean as a barometer for the planet, and Sophie (D) on history being reclaimed by the sea.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous to the mission?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) used his auditing skills, and Marcus (C) used his emergency dispatcher skills.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to buoyancy in currents or working in low visibility?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) mentions staying buoyant in currents, and Marcus (C) the lack of visibility.

questions.question: admits to having had prior doubts or having a strong emotional reaction to the return of wildlife?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) felt a sense of achievement seeing fish return, and Marcus (C) worried about his bravery.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about the ocean's resilience or its role as a record-keeper?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) saw the reef as a ledger of climate change, and Marcus (C) realized the ocean is forgiving.

questions.question: highlights the value of the expertise of others or the effective non-verbal communication?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) valued the biologists' expertise, and Sophie (D) the camaraderie and hand signals.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the unpleasant aspects of the task or the learning curve?
questions.explanation: Marcus (C) adjusted to the smell/weight of nets, and Sophie (D) found the measurement techniques frustrating.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external factor or opportunity that enabled their involvement?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) saw a social media post, and Sophie (D) received a university scholarship.
